Figure 3 The overall architecture of CausalDisenSeg. The framework is organized into three functional branches: (1) The Causal Stream (Top) extracts anatomical features (𝑐𝑀) via Ec to generate the segmentation mask (𝐷𝑠𝑒𝑔), reinforced by the Region Causality Module (RCM) to anchor attention on tumor regions. (2) The Bias Stream (Bottom) captures modality styles (𝑏𝑀) via
Table 2 Performance comparison (DSC%) on BraTS 2023 across 15 missing-modality settings.

Figure 1 Illustration of the main intuition behind CausalDisenSeg. Different MRI modalities present distinct imaging appearances but preserve a common tumor anatomy. The proposed framework seeks to capture this shared causal anatomy and reduce the influence of modality-specific bias, thereby improving robustness under missing modalities.
Table 4 Hyperparameter sensitivity results on BraTS 2023.

Figure 6 Heatmap visualization of causal features. The causal branch focuses on anatomically relevant tumor regions and boundaries across missing-modality settings, demonstrating anatomy-driven representation learning and highlighting the key advantage of the proposed causality-guided disentanglement framework.
Table 1 Performance comparison (DSC%) with SOTA methods on BraTS 2020. Available and missing modalities are denoted by ✓ and ✕, respectively.

Figure 5 t-SNE visualization of bias features before and after debiasing. Before debiasing, modality distributions are mixed with weak cluster structure; after debiasing, features form four distinct modality-specific clusters (T1(blue), T1ce(orange), T2(green), FLAIR (red)), showing improved style isolation in the bias branch.

Figure 4 Visual comparison across representative modality-availability settings in BRATS2023. Columns correspond to the input modalities, the predictions of CausalDisenSeg, PNDC, IM-Fuse, and the ground truth, respectively. Rows are arranged from sparse to complete input.
